A man has been charged in connection with the discovery of human remains in a duffel bag in Cambridge, Massachusetts. Carlos Colina, 32, faces charges of assault, battery, and improper disposal of a body. The remains belonged to Jonathan Camilien, 26, and police believe Colina knew the victim. Investigations are ongoing.

A man has been charged with assault and battery causing serious bodily injury and improper disposal of a body after human remains were found in a duffel bag in Cambridge, Massachusetts. The discovery was made over the weekend and led to the arrest of Carlos Colina, 32, who is accused of killing his friend, Jonathan Camilien, 26. The remains were found in two different locations and Colina is expected to appear in court on April 14.

A man, Carlos Colina, was charged with assault and improperly disposing of a body after human remains were found in a duffel bag in Cambridge, MA. The remains were identified as those of Jonathan Camilien, 26, who was known to the suspect. Police found more body parts in an apartment building near the police station.

Carlos Colina, 32, was charged with assault and battery causing serious bodily harm and improper disposal of a body after human remains were found in a duffel bag in Cambridge, Massachusetts. The remains belonged to 26-year-old Jonathan Camilien, who knew Colina, according to authorities.

After 11.5 hours of deliberation, a jury found Dzhokhar Tsarnaev guilty of all 30 counts he faced in the Boston Marathon bombing trial, 17 of which were capital charges, making him eligible for the death penalty. The trial will now move into a penalty phase where the jury will decide whether Tsarnaev will be executed. The jury will weigh factors such as the seriousness of his crimes against mitigating factors such as his family and mental health history. The start date of the penalty phase has not yet been set.

entities.text: a penalty phase where the jury will decide whether Tsarnaev will be executed

text: After deliberating for 11½ hours, jurors found Dzhokhar Tsarnaev guilty on all 30 counts he faced in the Boston Marathon bombing trial. Seventeen of the counts were capital charges, making him eligible for the death penalty. The trial will now move into a penalty phase, where the jury will decide whether Tsarnaev will be executed. The defense strategy focused on persuading the jury to spare Tsarnaev's life, arguing that his older brother was the instigator of the plot. Legal analysts believe the defense faces an uphill battle due to the severity and planning of the attack.

entities.text: all 30 counts he faced in the Boston Marathon bombing trial
